hero.subtitleLine1: Kwar-AI brings artificial intelligence to the center of disease surveillance.
hero.subtitleLine2: We help hospitals, researchers, and global health organizations anticipate outbreaks, automate analyses, and transform complex data into strategic intelligence.

cta.webSummit: Kwar-AI @ Web Summit Rio 2026

paragraphs: Inspired by the Tupi-Guarani term "Kuaray" — associated with the sun, cycles, and nature's signs — Kwar-AI was born to transform epidemiological data into operational intelligence for public health.
Epidemics don't start when they make the news. They leave traces earlier: in environmental, epidemiological, climatic, and behavioral data.

Our work is to transform these signs into operational intelligence. We combine epidemiology, mathematical modeling, and artificial intelligence to support faster, more strategic, and anticipatory decisions in public health.
Because epidemiological intelligence is not just about reacting to crises. It's about recognizing patterns before they become emergencies.
ourStory.quote: The sun rises for everyone. The difference lies in who can recognize the signs before dawn.

desafio.title:
Public health generates data at scale.
Turning it into decisions is still a challenge.
desafio.subtitle: Epidemiological databases, environmental indicators, notifications and hospital systems continue to grow. But integrating this information and turning it into actionable intelligence still requires time, technical infrastructure and analytical capacity.
painPoints: Fragmented data across multiple systems and institutions.
Slow analysis during outbreaks and critical situations.
Dependence on highly specialized technical teams.
Difficulty transforming information into coordinated action.

transition.title: We created EpidBot to make epidemiological intelligence accessible at scale.
transition.subtitle: A new interface for epidemiological analysis, integrating multiple data sources and transforming complex questions into actionable answers.

story.paragraph1: Long before algorithms existed, the Tupi people already understood something essential: the future leaves signs in the present. For them, Kuaray, the sun, was not just light, but cycle. Dawn was not a surprise; it was a consequence of patterns observed with attention.
story.paragraph2: The shamans didn't guess. They recognized signs. Before the rain, the wind changed. Before scarcity, movements altered. Before disease spread, the environment already indicated imbalance. There were signs — and there were those who knew how to read them.
story.kwarAiSuffix: is born from this same logic. It is a strategic light for public health decisions.
story.paragraph4: Epidemics don't start when they become news. They form silently, leaving traces in environmental, epidemiological and behavioral data. What seems like isolated noise can reveal a trend when analyzed together. Our work is to identify these convergences before they become crises.
story.paragraph5Suffix: has the conviction that outbreaks announce their own arrival. Epidemiological intelligence is the ability to recognize this announcement in time to act.
story.quote: The sun rises for everyone. But the

card.title: Ancestral Wisdom. Scientific Intelligence.
card.descriptionSuffix: combines the ancestral logic of observing signs with the rigor of statistical modeling and data science. It doesn't interpret omens — it analyzes environmental, epidemiological and behavioral data to turn signs into anticipatory and responsible decisions.

epidBot.title: EpidBot
epidBot.subtitle: Epidemiological intelligence in natural language.
epidBot.description:
EpidBot is an artificial intelligence assistant designed to explore epidemiological data using natural language.

You ask questions as you would to an analyst — and the system automatically generates analyzes, summaries and insights.

No programming knowledge or complex data analysis tools required.
epidBot.validationText: EpidBot is already being validated by epidemiologists, surveillance professionals and public health researchers — including the World Health Organization (WHO) Eastern Mediterranean Regional Office (EMRO).
epidBot.validationHighlight: World Health Organization (WHO) Eastern Mediterranean Regional Office (EMRO)

equipe.title: Who leads Kwar-AI
equipe.subtitle: Specialists with experience in research, epidemiological modeling, and development of applied solutions for public health.

flavio.name: Flávio Codeço Coelho
flavio.bio:
Professor at FGV EMAp • Creator of PySUS • Coordinator of InfoDengue and Mosqlimate projects.

Reference in mathematical modeling of epidemics, data science, and intelligent systems for public health surveillance. With over a decade of experience developing technologies and models applied to epidemiological monitoring in Brazil.

joyce.name: Joyce Figueiró Braga
joyce.bio:
PhD candidate in Applied Mathematics and Data Science at FGV EMAp • Product strategy and AI applied to public health.

Works at the intersection of artificial intelligence, epidemiological modeling, and building products oriented toward real impact. Combines analytical vision, strategic communication, and experience in transforming scientific knowledge into accessible and applicable solutions.

equipeRede.description: Today, Kwar-AI is driven by a founding team with solid scientific and technological foundation. To develop and apply solutions in public health, we work with researchers, developers and partner institutions in Brazil and abroad.

hero.titleLine2: for you to analyze the data.
hero.subtitle: Your team is overwhelmed. And the problem won't wait.

problem.title: You try, but...
problem.subtitle: Being a health secretary in a small town means taking care of many things with few people.

team.title: Your team is already at the limit
team.description: You have 2 or 3 people doing the work of 10. Everyone is overwhelmed. And you still need to stop to gather data from many different places.

surprise.title: The disease spreads and you only see it later
surprise.description: When the report is finally ready, cases have already increased. The outbreak has already spread. And you're finding out too late where you should have acted before.

money.title: You lose money by not planning
money.description: Did you open beds you didn't need? Buy too many vaccines? Fail to buy what was missing? Without clear data, you waste resources your city doesn't have to spare.
